_meta.title: Contaminant → cited health-impact knowledge base
_meta.note: Every statement is sourced. The Health-Impact agent may only emit sentences grounded in this KB; the Verifier rejects anything uncited.
sources: WHO Guidelines for Drinking-water Quality (4th ed.)

arsenic.label: Arsenic (As)
arsenic.summary: Long-term ingestion of arsenic above the limit causes skin lesions, and is linked to cancers of the skin, bladder and lung, and to cardiovascular disease. Effects are chronic and cumulative.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed. — Arsenic: carcinogenic; chronic exposure causes skin lesions and internal cancers

fluoride.label: Fluoride (F)
fluoride.summary: Fluoride above 1.5 mg/L causes dental fluorosis (mottling of teeth); prolonged high exposure causes skeletal fluorosis — stiffness, joint pain and bone deformity. Endemic across parts of India.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed. — Fluoride: dental and skeletal fluorosis above guideline value

nitrate.label: Nitrate (NO3)
nitrate.summary: High nitrate is dangerous for bottle-fed infants — it causes methaemoglobinaemia ('blue-baby syndrome'), reducing the blood's ability to carry oxygen. The 45 mg/L limit has no relaxation.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed. — Nitrate/Nitrite: methaemoglobinaemia risk in infants

lead.label: Lead (Pb)
lead.summary: Lead is a cumulative poison with no safe level. In children it impairs brain development and lowers IQ; in adults it raises blood pressure and harms the kidneys. The limit has no relaxation.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed. — Lead: neurodevelopmental harm in children; no safe threshold

total_coliform.label: Total Coliform Bacteria
total_coliform.summary: Coliform bacteria indicate the water is microbiologically unsafe and may carry pathogens. BIS requires they be absent in any 100 mL sample. Presence signals a contamination pathway.

citation.reference: IS 10500:2012 Clause 5.2 — coliforms shall not be detectable; WHO microbial guidance

e_coli.label: E. coli
e_coli.summary: E. coli is direct evidence of faecal contamination and a high risk of waterborne disease — diarrhoea, cholera, typhoid and hepatitis. Any detectable count is unsafe; treat the water before drinking.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed. — E. coli: indicator of faecal contamination and enteric pathogen risk

iron.label: Iron (Fe)
iron.summary: Iron above 0.3 mg/L is mainly an aesthetic problem — metallic taste, reddish-brown staining of laundry and fixtures. It is not an acute health hazard at typical levels but makes water unpalatable.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed. — Iron: aesthetic (taste/staining), no health-based guideline

tds.label: Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)
tds.summary: High TDS affects taste (salty/bitter) and indicates high mineral content. Within the permissible band it is mainly a palatability issue; very high TDS can cause gastrointestinal irritation.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed. — TDS: palatability and aesthetic acceptability

total_hardness.label: Total Hardness
total_hardness.summary: Hard water is not a health hazard; it causes scaling in pipes and appliances, reduces soap lather, and can taste chalky. Very soft water may be more corrosive to plumbing.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed. — Hardness: no health-based guideline; aesthetic/scaling effects

turbidity.label: Turbidity
turbidity.summary: Turbidity is suspended particles that cloud the water. It is an indicator: high turbidity shields microbes from disinfection and signals possible contamination, so it must be cleared before disinfection.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed. — Turbidity: indicator affecting disinfection efficacy

ph.label: pH
ph.summary: Water outside pH 6.5–8.5 can be corrosive (low pH, leaching metals from pipes) or scale-forming (high pH) and reduces disinfection efficiency. pH itself has no direct health-based guideline.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed. — pH: operational parameter affecting corrosion and disinfection

chloride.label: Chloride (Cl)
chloride.summary: High chloride imparts a salty taste and can accelerate corrosion of plumbing. It is an aesthetic parameter with no direct health-based limit at the concentrations seen in drinking water.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed. — Chloride: taste and corrosion, aesthetic

sulphate.label: Sulphate (SO4)
sulphate.summary: Sulphate above ~250 mg/L can cause a noticeable taste and, at high levels, a temporary laxative effect, especially in people not accustomed to it.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed. — Sulphate: taste and laxative effect at high concentration

manganese.label: Manganese (Mn)
manganese.summary: Excess manganese stains fixtures and laundry black/brown and affects taste; very high chronic exposure has been associated with neurological effects.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed. — Manganese: aesthetic staining; possible neurological effect at high exposure

aluminium.label: Aluminium (Al)
aluminium.summary: Aluminium in drinking water is mainly an operational/aesthetic concern (from coagulation), causing discolouration. It is kept low as a quality-of-treatment indicator.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed. — Aluminium: operational parameter from treatment

copper.label: Copper (Cu)
copper.summary: High copper, often from corroding pipes, causes a bitter taste and gastrointestinal upset (nausea, stomach cramps) on short-term exposure.

citation.reference: WHO GDWQ 4th ed. — Copper: gastrointestinal effects on acute exposure

_default.label: Parameter
_default.summary: This parameter exceeds the BIS desirable limit. Its specific health impact is not in the curated knowledge base, so no health claim is asserted beyond the measured exceedance.

citation.reference: IS 10500:2012 — exceedance recorded against the standard; no unsourced health claim made
